title: The Living Root Bridges of Meghalaya: A First-Timer's Trekking Guide
excerpt: The double-decker living root bridge at Nongriat is one of the most extraordinary structures in Asia — grown, not built, over centuries by the Khasi people. Here's everything you need to know before you make the descent.

sections.heading: What living root bridges actually are
sections.body: The living root bridges of Meghalaya are not constructed — they are trained. Over generations, the Khasi and Jaintia peoples have guided the aerial roots of the Indian rubber tree (Ficus elastica) across rivers and gorges, interlacing them around bamboo and wooden scaffolds until the roots fuse and harden into load-bearing spans. The process takes 15 to 30 years for a basic single-span bridge and significantly longer for the famous double-decker at Nongriat. Unlike timber bridges, these structures grow stronger with age; some in the East Khasi Hills are estimated to be over 500 years old and support the weight of 50 people at once. Walking across one is a genuinely disorienting experience — the roots flex slightly underfoot and the whole structure is alive.

sections.heading: The Nongriat trek: 3,500 steps and worth every one
sections.body: The trailhead begins at Tyrna village, reached by a shared taxi from Cherrapunji (Sohra) — roughly 12 km on a winding hill road. From Tyrna, a stone staircase descends approximately 3,500 steps into the valley, dropping around 700 metres in elevation over 4 kilometres. The descent takes 90 minutes at a moderate pace; the ascent on the return takes 2 to 2.5 hours and is the part that catches most first-timers off guard. The double-decker bridge sits in the valley at Nongriat village, 30 minutes beyond the first single bridge you encounter. Start by 7 AM from Cherrapunji to reach the bridge before midday heat and afternoon rain, which arrive almost daily between April and October.

sections.heading: Where to stay: Nongriat overnight or day trip from Cherrapunji
sections.body: The most rewarding way to do the Nongriat trek is to stay overnight in the valley. Two or three basic guesthouses operate in Nongriat village — Niwan's Nongriat Guesthouse and Rishot's are the most consistently recommended — charging ₹400–600 per night for a simple room with meals. Staying overnight means you have the bridge entirely to yourself in the early morning before the day-trippers arrive, and you can explore the smaller root bridges and natural swimming pools in the valley at your own pace. If a day trip is all you have time for, stay in Cherrapunji itself; hotels like Polo Orchid Resort and Cherrapunjee Holiday Resort sit at the plateau edge with cloud views and run organised transfers to Tyrna.

sections.heading: What to carry on the descent
sections.body: The Nongriat trek is not technically demanding but it is physically taxing, and the steps are uneven stone in high-humidity jungle. Wear proper trekking shoes or rubber-soled trail runners with ankle support — flip-flops are a poor choice and sandals are dangerous on the wet steps. Carry at least 2 litres of water per person; a small shop at the base of the valley sells packaged water but stock is unreliable. Pack a rain jacket even in winter months, as Cherrapunji receives rain year-round. Trekking poles significantly reduce knee strain on the ascent. Carry cash — no ATMs exist in the valley and Nongriat is beyond mobile network range for most operators except BSNL.

sections.heading: Best season and what to expect beyond the bridge
sections.body: October through April is the best window for the Nongriat trek — the post-monsoon forest is lush, the water levels in the natural pools are perfect for swimming, and the humidity is comparatively manageable. June through September sees the heaviest rainfall on the planet (Cherrapunji and Mawsynram regularly record the world's highest annual rainfall figures), and the steps become slippery and occasionally dangerous. Beyond the double-decker bridge, the trail continues to Rainbow Falls — a 3-kilometre walk through dense forest that rewards with a waterfall plunging into a turquoise pool. Few day-trippers make it this far, which means you will likely have it to yourself. Meghalaya's circuit pairs naturally with Shillong (2.5 hours from Cherrapunji) and the Dawki river on the Bangladesh border.

title: Hornbill Festival 2026: How to Plan the Trip Right
excerpt: The Hornbill Festival brings all of Nagaland's 16 major tribes together in one place for ten days each December. It's one of India's greatest cultural spectacles — and one of the most under-planned trips people take.

sections.heading: Dates, venue, and the basic shape of the festival
sections.body: The Hornbill Festival runs from 1 to 10 December every year at the Naga Heritage Village in Kisama, 12 kilometres south of Kohima on the Dimapur–Kohima highway. The site is an open-air heritage park where each of Nagaland's 16 major tribes maintains a traditional morung (bachelor's dormitory) — and during the festival, those morungs become performance stages, food stalls, and craft exhibitions. Entry is ticketed: ₹200 per day for Indian nationals, ₹500 per day for foreigners, and multi-day passes are available at the gate. The first and last days tend to draw the largest crowds; the middle days (4–7 December) offer a better balance of programming and space.

sections.heading: ILP permits: sort this first, everything else second
sections.body: Nagaland is an Inner Line Permit (ILP) state, and you cannot enter the state without one — this applies to all Indian nationals (and foreigners require a Protected Area Permit instead, applied through the Ministry of Home Affairs). Indian citizens can obtain an ILP through the Nagaland Resident Commissioner offices in Delhi, Kolkata, Shillong, or Guwahati, or via the official Nagaland e-ILP portal at nagaland-ilp.in, which processes applications within 24–48 hours. The permit must be printed and carried at all times; checkpoints at the state border and at Dimapur airport verify it. Apply at least a week before travel — the portal experiences heavy traffic in the November run-up to Hornbill.

sections.heading: Getting to Kohima: flights, trains, and the road
sections.body: Dimapur is Nagaland's only commercial airport with connections to Kolkata, Guwahati, and Delhi (via IndiGo and Air India). From Dimapur, Kohima is a 74-kilometre drive that takes 3 to 3.5 hours on the winding mountain highway — shared taxis depart from Dimapur bus stand throughout the day and cost ₹300–350 per seat. Trains to Dimapur run from Guwahati (5 hours, overnight options available), making Guwahati a practical hub if you plan to combine the festival with Kaziranga or Meghalaya. Roads from Dimapur to Kohima during the festival period are congested; leave before 8 AM or after 7 PM to avoid the worst jams.

sections.heading: Where to stay in Kohima during the festival
sections.body: Book accommodation four to six months in advance — this cannot be overstated. Every guesthouse and hotel in Kohima fills completely for Hornbill, and prices roughly double from normal rates. Japfu Hotel and Hotel Saramati are the most reliable mid-range options close to the town centre. For something closer to the festival ground, several families in Kisama village rent rooms during the festival period; these are advertised on the Nagaland Tourism Facebook page and the Hornbill Festival official website. Staying in Kisama means you can walk to the venue rather than joining the daily vehicle crush on the highway. Camp Hornbill, run annually near the venue, offers tented accommodation and is popular with younger travellers.

sections.heading: What to eat and buy: making the most of the grounds
sections.body: The food pavilions at Kisama are a parallel festival unto themselves. Each tribal morung serves food specific to their community — the Ao Naga stall is known for smoked pork with fermented bamboo shoot, the Lotha section for anishi (coiled yam leaves fermented with pork fat), and the Chakhesang morung for zutho, a traditional millet beer served in bamboo cups. Eating your way through the morungs is one of the great pleasures of Hornbill. The craft stalls sell handwoven shawls (each tribe has distinct patterns that carry social meaning), handmade jewellery, and traditional weapons; prices are fixed rather than negotiable. Carry enough cash — card machines at the venue are limited.

title: Kaziranga Safari Guide: Jeep vs Elephant, Zones, and the Best Light
excerpt: Kaziranga is home to two-thirds of the world's one-horned rhinoceros population and some of the most accessible wildlife viewing in Asia. Knowing which zone to book and when to go changes everything.

sections.heading: Season and park access: November to April only
sections.body: Kaziranga National Park closes every year during monsoon season, typically from May to October, when the Brahmaputra floods large sections of the park and wildlife disperses to the Karbi Anglong hills to the south. The park reopens in November and the best wildlife density is December through March, when the tall elephant grass has been trimmed by seasonal burning and animals concentrate near the remaining water bodies. April is the last month before closure and offers the hottest temperatures but also the most dramatic landscapes as the grass burns. Book jeep safaris well in advance for December and January, which are peak tourist months.

sections.heading: The three main zones: Central, Western, and Eastern
sections.body: Kaziranga is divided into four ranges; the three most accessible to visitors are the Central (Kohora), Western (Bagori), and Eastern (Agoratoli) ranges. The Central Range is the most visited and offers the highest rhino density — it is the range most frequently photographed and the one to prioritise if you have only one safari. The Western Range is excellent for elephant herds and has more open grassland; tiger sightings here are relatively more frequent than in the Central Range. The Eastern Range sees far fewer tourists and offers a genuinely wilder experience, with large water buffalo herds and excellent birdwatching along the Aie River. Book the Central Range for your first visit; add Western or Eastern if you have additional days.

sections.heading: Jeep safari vs elephant safari: the real difference
sections.body: Jeep safaris and elephant safaris serve fundamentally different purposes in Kaziranga. The morning jeep safari (departing at sunrise, around 5:30–6:00 AM depending on season) covers more ground — up to 20 kilometres through the park — and gives you sustained time with a wide variety of animals. Elephant safaris are 45-minute rides on trained park elephants that take you into tall grass where jeeps cannot go, often getting you within 20 metres of rhinos in ways that would be impossible otherwise. The elephant safari is a unique experience but covers very little ground. Both are worth doing if your schedule allows; most repeat Kaziranga visitors describe the elephant safari as the more intimate encounter but the jeep safari as the more complete wildlife experience.

sections.heading: Photography and light: the windows that matter
sections.body: The hour after sunrise and the hour before sunset are the photographic windows in Kaziranga. Midday light is harsh and most wildlife retreats to shade or water, making the 10 AM to 3 PM period the quietest for sightings. Morning safaris in the Central Range consistently offer the best light conditions — the Brahmaputra floodplain creates a low-lying mist that burns off slowly and produces extraordinary golden-hour conditions against which rhinos and elephants stand in striking silhouette. A 300mm telephoto lens is the minimum useful reach for frame-filling wildlife shots from a jeep; many photographers prefer 400–500mm. If you are shooting with a smartphone, the elephant safari's proximity advantage is worth more than any focal length.

sections.heading: Staying near the park: where to base yourself
sections.body: Kohora village, adjacent to the Central Range entrance, is the most practical base and has the widest accommodation range from budget to luxury. Iora — The Retreat and Diphlu River Lodge are the most atmospheric options, both with naturalist guides and direct park views; book these three to four months ahead. For budget travellers, several guesthouses in Kohora charge ₹800–1,500 per night. Avoid staying in Jorhat (53 km away) unless you have specific reasons — the early morning safari departures make the distance genuinely inconvenient. Kaziranga pairs well with a transit through Guwahati (250 km west) or an onward journey to Majuli island (120 km east).

title: 7 Days in Arunachal: The Tawang Itinerary We Actually Run
excerpt: The road to Tawang is one of the most dramatic drives in the world — through Bhalukpong, past Dirang's apple orchards, over Sela Pass at 13,700 feet, and into a valley of monasteries above the clouds. Here's the itinerary, hour by hour.

sections.heading: ILP permits and entry logistics: this comes before everything
sections.body: Arunachal Pradesh is an Inner Line Permit state, and the permit is non-negotiable before entry. Indian nationals can apply online at arunachalilp.com — the process takes 24 to 48 hours and the permit must be printed and carried throughout the trip. Foreign nationals require a Protected Area Permit (PAP) and Restricted Area Permit (RAP) in addition to an ILP; the process is more involved and requires applying through a registered tour operator at least four weeks in advance. The standard entry point for the Tawang circuit is Bhalukpong, where the ILP is checked on the bridge crossing into the state. Ensure your permit lists Tawang specifically as the destination, not just West Kameng district.

sections.heading: Day 1–2: Bhalukpong to Dirang, acclimatising on the way
sections.body: Bhalukpong sits at 213 metres above sea level; Tawang is at 3,048 metres. Driving straight through in one day is a common mistake that causes altitude sickness. The itinerary we run stops in Dirang (1,560 metres) for the first night, which gives the body a gentle step-up. Dirang is a small, pleasant village in the Kameng river valley, surrounded by apple orchards that are spectacular in spring (March to April) and harvest season (September to October). Mandala Farmstay and Hotel Pemaling are good mid-range options. On the drive from Bhalukpong to Dirang (6–7 hours), stop at Bomdila for lunch and visit the Bomdila Monastery; the town has views back into the Assam plains that make it worth the 30-minute detour.

sections.heading: Day 3: Sela Pass and the descent into Tawang
sections.body: The drive from Dirang to Tawang via Sela Pass is 100 kilometres and takes 5 to 6 hours. Leave by 6 AM to clear the pass before afternoon fog and potential snowfall, which can close the road without warning. Sela Pass at 4,170 metres is the journey's highest point; Para Lake beside it freezes solid in winter and reflects the sky in October. The pass is cold even in May — carry a warm layer regardless of the season. The descent from Sela into the Tawang valley is one of the most extraordinary drives in India: 40 kilometres of hairpin turns through cloud forest until the valley opens and you see Tawang Monastery's ochre walls against the ridge. Check into your hotel before 4 PM; altitude headaches are common on the first night in Tawang.

sections.heading: Day 4–5: Tawang town and the valleys beyond
sections.body: Tawang Monastery — the largest Buddhist monastery in India and second-largest in the world after Lhasa's Potala Palace — takes a full morning to explore properly. The main prayer hall contains a 8-metre golden statue of Buddha and the monastery's library holds thousands of ancient manuscripts. The town below the monastery has changed significantly in the past decade: cafes, guesthouses, and a small market now line the main street, and the war memorial at the bottom of the hill offers context on the 1962 Sino-Indian conflict. On day five, drive to Bumla Pass on the China border (37 km from Tawang, accessible with permits arranged through the district administration), Madhuri Lake (formerly known as Shungetser Lake), and the Nuranang Waterfall — all within a single day circuit from Tawang.

sections.heading: Day 6–7: Return via Nameri and the logistics of the journey
sections.body: The return journey mirrors the ascent but consider varying the route by stopping at Nameri National Park near Bhalukpong for a final night — a buffer before catching a flight or train from Guwahati or Tezpur. Nameri's river safaris on the Jia Bhorali offer birdwatching for Bengal Florican and Great Hornbill and serve as a gentle decompression after the high altitude. The nearest airport to the Tawang circuit is Tezpur (IATA: TEI), connected to Kolkata and Guwahati; Guwahati airport offers more onward connections. The total driving distance for the full circuit — Guwahati to Tawang and back via the same route — is approximately 1,400 kilometres; a hired driver who knows the mountain roads is significantly preferable to self-driving.

title: What to Eat in the North East: From Smoked Pork to Jadoh
excerpt: North East India has one of the most diverse and least-documented food cultures in Asia. If you're arriving expecting standard Indian restaurant food, you're in for a complete recalibration.

sections.heading: Khasi jadoh: Meghalaya's red rice at the heart of everything
sections.body: Jadoh is the foundational dish of Khasi cuisine — red rice cooked with pork (or sometimes fish) in pork fat, seasoned with ginger, turmeric, and black sesame. The result is aromatic without being spiced in the way that most Indian food is; the flavour comes from the quality of the pork and the earthiness of the red rice, not from chilli heat. Jadoh is eaten at every meal in Meghalaya and served at market stalls from dawn. The best place to try it is at Iewduh (Bara Bazaar) in Shillong, where stalls near the meat section serve freshly cooked jadoh alongside doh khlieh (pork and onion salad with raw ginger) and tungtap (a pungent dried fish chutney that is an acquired taste but essential to the meal).

sections.heading: Naga smoked pork with bamboo shoot: fermentation as flavour
sections.body: Naga cuisine is built around two techniques: smoking and fermenting. The smoked pork that Nagaland is famous for is not cured like European bacon — it is fire-smoked over several days until deeply flavoured and preserved, then cooked with fermented bamboo shoot (which has a sour, almost cheesy intensity) and dried chillies, the Bhut jolokia among them. The dish is incendiary by most standards but the smokiness and sourness balance the heat in a way that makes it deeply addictive. Anishi — dried colocasia leaves fermented with pork fat — is another Naga staple with an intensely funky flavour that divides visitors cleanly into converts and those who stick to the rice. Both dishes appear at Hornbill Festival food stalls and in restaurants in Kohima's main market area.

sections.heading: Assamese thali: the river's pantry on a plate
sections.body: An Assamese thali reflects the state's geography: Brahmaputra river fish (rohu and hilsa cooked with mustard), bamboo shoot pickle, khar (an alkaline preparation made with banana peel ash, unlike anything else in Indian cooking), steamed rice, and masor tenga — a sour fish curry made with elephant apple or tomatoes. The meal is lighter than Bengali food and relies on bitterness, sourness, and fermentation for complexity rather than spice. Traditionally, every Assamese meal ends with a digestive called tamul-pan — betel nut sliced thin and folded in a betel leaf with lime paste — offered by the host. Eating a proper Assamese thali at a home-style restaurant in Jorhat or Guwahati is a different experience from hotel dining; look for places where construction workers eat lunch.

sections.heading: Apatani kitchens of Ziro: the food of a UNESCO-nominated landscape
sections.body: The Apatani tribe of Ziro valley in Arunachal Pradesh practice an integrated farming system — fish raised in the channels between rice paddies, millet grown on the edges — that UNESCO has repeatedly nominated as world heritage. Their food reflects this: fresh water fish eaten simply, sometimes raw with ginger and herbs; pork prepared with indigenous herbs including the distinctively aromatic ngoceen leaf; and rice beer (apong) that is central to every communal occasion. Ziro's small guesthouses and homestays, run increasingly by younger Apatani families, are the only practical way to eat this food — it exists almost nowhere outside the valley. The annual Ziro Music Festival (September) brings the food culture into focus for a brief period each year.

sections.heading: Manipuri eromba and the vegetable-forward kitchen
sections.body: Manipuri cuisine challenges the assumption that North East food is primarily about meat. Eromba — the state's essential dish — is a mash of boiled vegetables (typically yam, beans, or colocasia) combined with fermented fish (ngari) and roasted dried chillies into a dense, umami-rich paste. The fermented fish smell is assertive and unforgettable; the flavour once tasted disappears into the body's memory permanently. Singju, a fresh salad of shredded raw vegetables tossed with ngari, smoked fish, or lotus stem, is eaten as a snack or starter and demonstrates how the Meitei kitchen handles vegetables with as much sophistication as the meat preparations. Imphal's Ima Keithel (Mothers' Market), run entirely by women, is the best single place in the North East to encounter the full range of local produce and prepared foods in one sprawling, extraordinary space.

title: Majuli: Slow Travel on the World's Largest River Island
excerpt: Majuli sits in the middle of the Brahmaputra in upper Assam — a flat, cycling-perfect island of monasteries, mask-makers, and migratory birds, slowly being reclaimed by one of the world's most powerful rivers.

sections.heading: Getting to Majuli: the ferry from Nimati Ghat
sections.body: Access to Majuli is by ferry only — the island has no road or rail bridge to the mainland. The primary crossing is from Nimati Ghat, 14 kilometres from Jorhat on the south bank of the Brahmaputra; government ferries depart at roughly 10 AM and 1 PM daily, with the crossing taking 1 to 1.5 hours depending on the river's current and sandbar configuration. The ferry carries passengers, motorcycles, and small vehicles; book your ferry boarding in advance during the October–November peak season when visitor numbers spike. A private speedboat service also operates from Nimati Ghat and takes 30 minutes, but at considerably higher cost. From Jorhat, Nimati Ghat is reached by local bus or auto-rickshaw. The ferry experience itself — watching the Brahmaputra's vast, brown expanse widen around you until the island's treeline appears — sets the mood for everything that follows.

sections.heading: Satras: the Vaishnavite monasteries that define the island
sections.body: Majuli is the cultural heart of Assamese neo-Vaishnavism, the devotional tradition founded by the 15th-century saint-scholar Srimanta Sankardeva. The island's satras — monastery-villages that function simultaneously as religious centres, performance schools, and community institutions — are Majuli's primary cultural draw. Kamalabari Satra, Auniati Satra, and Garamur Satra are the most accessible and all welcome respectful visitors. Auniati Satra maintains a museum of traditional Assamese artefacts including ancient jewellery, utensils, and manuscripts. The satras perform Sattriya dance and Borgeet devotional music during morning and evening prayer; arriving in time for a prayer session is easy with an overnight stay on the island and transforms the visit from sightseeing into something more resonant.

sections.heading: Mask-making: the living craft of Chamaguri Satra
sections.body: Chamaguri Satra is the centre of Majuli's traditional mask-making tradition — a craft that produces the enormous, painted masks used in Ankiya Nat (Vaishnavite theatre plays). The masks are made from bamboo, clay, cloth, and wood pulp, some reaching two metres in height and depicting characters from Ramayana and Mahabharata mythology. The artisans at Chamaguri welcome visitors and are generally willing to explain the construction process; small masks are available for purchase and are among the most distinctive crafts from anywhere in North East India. Photography is permitted. Bring cash — the satra does not have card facilities and the nearest ATM is in Garamur town, a 20-minute cycle away.

sections.heading: Cycling the island: the right way to move
sections.body: Majuli's flat terrain, quiet roads, and small scale (roughly 70 km across at its widest) make it ideal for cycling, and renting a bicycle is the universally recommended way to explore. Guesthouses in Kamalabari and Garamur rent bicycles for ₹100–150 per day. A full-island circuit passing all the major satras, the egret colony at Salmora Ghat, and the north shore erosion banks takes 6 to 7 hours at a comfortable pace with stops. Early morning is the best time to cycle — the light on the rice fields and the activity at the satras is concentrated before 9 AM. Carry water, a rain jacket, and sunscreen; the island has no large pharmacy or well-stocked store, and the ferry back departs on a strict schedule.

sections.heading: The erosion context: what slow travel means on a disappearing island
sections.body: Majuli's story cannot be separated from the Brahmaputra's power. The island measured 1,246 square kilometres in 1991; today the figure is approximately 523 square kilometres, with satellite images showing visible shrinkage every monsoon. Annual flooding inundates 70 to 80 percent of the island, and entire villages have been relocated multiple times in living memory. The residents of Majuli are neither defeated by this nor in denial of it; the satras have moved their buildings, communities have adapted their farming cycles, and life continues with a pragmatism that is itself worth understanding. Visiting Majuli with awareness of this context — asking guesthouse owners about the flooding, noticing the embankment walls and sand deposits — makes the island's determined cultural vitality significantly more moving.
